learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Greedy Algorithm, Loops, Time Complexity, Dynamic Programming Basics
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine crossing a river using stepping stones. Each stone tells you the maximum distance you can jump ahead. Your goal is to determine whether you can reach the final stone without getting stuck.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine crossing a river using stepping stones. Each stone tells you the maximum distance you can jump ahead. Your goal is to determine whether you can reach the final stone without getting stuck.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this Greedy Reachability pattern whenever each position extends your reachable range and you only need to determine whether the destination is reachable.
learning.signals: Whenever every position expands the reachable range and you only need to determine whether the destination is reachable, think of Greedy Reachability.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: You are given an integer array nums. Each element nums[i] represents the maximum jump length from that position. Starting from the first index, determine whether you can reach the last index.

examples.explanation: Jump from index 0 to index 1, then to the last index.
Index 3 blocks further movement, making the last index unreachable.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Try every possible jump recursively until reaching the last index.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Instead of deciding the exact jump at every position, simply keep track of the farthest index you can currently reach. If your current position is beyond that reach, then continuing is impossible.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Only the farthest reachable position matters; the exact path is unnecessary.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever every position expands the reachable range and you only need to determine whether the destination is reachable, think of Greedy Reachability.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Each index is processed only once while maintaining the maximum reachable position. No unnecessary paths are explored, making it an optimal greedy solution.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever every position expands the reachable range and you only need to determine whether the destination is reachable, think of Greedy Reachability.
commonWrongPaths: Confusing maximum jump with exact jump.
Stopping before checking all reachable positions.

hints: If the current index is unreachable, stop immediately.
commonMisconceptions: Confusing maximum jump with exact jump.
Stopping before checking all reachable positions.

pattern.name: Greedy Reachability
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever every position expands the reachable range and you only need to determine whether the destination is reachable, think of Greedy Reachability.

approaches.title: Recursive Backtracking
approaches.idea: Try every possible jump recursively until reaching the last index.
algorithm: Try every jump from 1 to nums[i].
Return true if any path reaches the end.
approaches.dryRun: Index 0 → Try all possible jumps recursively.

code.java: Recursive DFS exploring every possible jump.

approaches.title: Dynamic Programming
approaches.idea: Mark indices that can reach the last index using memoization or tabulation.
algorithm: Mark indices that can reach a good position.
approaches.dryRun:
Mark last index good.
Propagate backwards.

approaches.title: Greedy Reachability
approaches.idea: Maintain the farthest index reachable while traversing the array. If the current index is ever greater than the farthest reachable index, return false. Otherwise, continuously update the farthest reachable position.
approaches.intuition: Each index is processed only once while maintaining the maximum reachable position. No unnecessary paths are explored, making it an optimal greedy solution.
algorithm: If current index > farthest, return false.
If farthest reaches the last index, return true.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[2,3,1,1,4]
Reach=2
Reach=4
Last index reachable.

visualization.description: Keep expanding the farthest reachable position.
steps: If current index exceeds reach, stop.

interviewFollowUps: How is this different from Jump Game II?
Why does the greedy solution work?
Can you reconstruct one valid path?
What if jumps could be negative?
interviewTips: Show why only the farthest reachable index matters.
Explain why greedy never misses a valid solution.
State O(n) time and O(1) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Only the farthest reachable position matters; the exact path is unnecessary.

commonMistakes: Confusing maximum jump with exact jump.
Stopping before checking all reachable positions.
